subject: Physics
q: Two cars $A$ and $B$ each of mass $10^{3} \mathrm{~kg}$ are moving on parallel tracks separated by a distance of 10 m, in same direction with speeds $72 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h}$ and $36 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h}$. The magnitude of angular momentum of $\operatorname{car} A$ with respect to $\operatorname{car} B$ is $\_\_\_\_$ J.s.
question: Two cars $A$ and $B$ each of mass $10^{3} \mathrm{~kg}$ are moving on parallel tracks separated by a distance of 10 m, in same direction with speeds $72 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h}$ and $36 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h}$. The magnitude of angular momentum of $\operatorname{car} A$ with respect to $\operatorname{car} B$ is $\_\_\_\_$ J.s.
solution:
Mass of each car $m = 10^3$ kg.
Speed of car $A$, $v_A = 72$ km/h $= 72 \times \frac{5}{18} = 20$ m/s.
Speed of car $B$, $v_B = 36$ km/h $= 36 \times \frac{5}{18} = 10$ m/s.
Relative velocity of car $A$ with respect to car $B$ is $v_{AB} = v_A - v_B = 20 - 10 = 10$ m/s.
The cars are moving on parallel tracks separated by a perpendicular distance $d = 10$ m.
The magnitude of angular momentum of car $A$ with respect to car $B$ is given by $L = m \times v_{AB} \times d$.
Substituting the values: $L = 10^3 \times 10 \times 10 = 10^5$ J.s.
Thus, the correct option is (2).

subject: Physics
q: A rectangular solid box of length 0.3 m is held horizontally, with one of its sides on the edge of a platform of height 5 m . When released, it slips off the table in a very short time τ=0.01 s , remaining essentially horizontal. The angle by which it would rotate when it hits the ground will be (in radians) close to:
question: A rectangular solid box of length 0.3 m is held horizontally, with one of its sides on the edge of a platform of height 5 m . When released, it slips off the table in a very short time τ=0.01 s , remaining essentially horizontal. The angle by which it would rotate when it hits the ground will be (in radians) close to:
solution:
Angular impulse = change in angular momentum
τΔt=ΔL
mgl2×0.01=ml23ω
ω=3g×0.012l=3×10×0.012×0.3=0.5 rad/sec
Time taken by rod to hit the ground = 2hg=2×510=1 s
Angle rotated by rod = ωt=0.5×1=0.5 rad

question: A solid sphere and solid cylinder of identical radii approach an incline with the same linear velocity (see figure). Both roll without slipping all throughout. The two climb maximum heights hsph and hcyl on the inline. The ratio hsphhcyl  is given by:
solution:
From conservation of mechanical energy,
For solid sphere: 12msphV2+12Isphω2=msphghsph
V22+V25=ghsph 
7V210=ghsph …(i)
For cylinder: 12mcylV2+12Icylω2=mcylghcyl
V22+V24=ghcyl
3V24=ghcyl …(ii)
From (i) and (ii) hsphhcyl=1415

subject: Physics
q: Moment of inertia about an axis $AB$ for a rod of mass $40$ kg and length $3$ m is same as that of a solid sphere of mass of $10$ kg and radius $R$ about an axis parallel to $AB$ axis with separation of $3$ m as shown in figure below. The value of $R$ is given as $\sqrt{\dfrac{\alpha}{2}}$. The value of $\alpha$ is _______.
question: Moment of inertia about an axis $AB$ for a rod of mass $40$ kg and length $3$ m is same as that of a solid sphere of mass of $10$ kg and radius $R$ about an axis parallel to $AB$ axis with separation of $3$ m as shown in figure below. The value of $R$ is given as $\sqrt{\dfrac{\alpha}{2}}$. The value of $\alpha$ is _______.
solution:
Moment of inertia of the rod about axis $AB$ passing through one of its ends:
$I_{\text{rod}} = \dfrac{ML^2}{3} = \dfrac{40 \times (3)^2}{3} = \dfrac{40 \times 9}{3} = 120\text{ kg}\cdot\text{m}^2$

Moment of inertia of the solid sphere about the same axis $AB$, using the parallel axis theorem $I = I_{\text{cm}} + md^2$:
Mass $m = 10\text{ kg}$, radius $R$, distance from the centre of the sphere to axis $AB$ is $d = 3\text{ m}$.

$I_{\text{sphere}} = \dfrac{2}{5}mR^2 + md^2 = \dfrac{2}{5}(10)R^2 + 10(3)^2 = 4R^2 + 90$

Equating the two moments of inertia:
$120 = 4R^2 + 90$
$4R^2 = 30$
$R^2 = \dfrac{15}{2}$
$R = \sqrt{\dfrac{15}{2}}$

Comparing with $R = \sqrt{\dfrac{\alpha}{2}}$:
$\alpha = 15$

Hence, $\alpha = 15$.

subject: Physics
q: As shown in the figure, a bob of mass m is tied to a massless string whose other end portion is wound on a fly wheel (disc) of radius r and mass m. When released from rest the bob starts falling vertically. When it has covered a distance of h, the angular speed of the wheel will be:
question: As shown in the figure, a bob of mass m is tied to a massless string whose other end portion is wound on a fly wheel (disc) of radius r and mass m. When released from rest the bob starts falling vertically. When it has covered a distance of h, the angular speed of the wheel will be:
solution:
Using work-energy theorem 

mgh=12mv2+12Iω2

mgh=12mω2r2+12mr22ω2    ( since I=mr22 and v=rω )

mgh=34mω2r2

Hence, ω=4gh3r2=1r4gh3

subject: Physics
q: A thin solid disk of 1 kg is rotating along its diameter axis at the speed of 1800 rpm. By applying an external torque of $25 \pi \mathrm{Nm}$ for 40 s, the speed increases to 2100 rpm. The diameter of the disk is ________ m.
question: A thin solid disk of 1 kg is rotating along its diameter axis at the speed of 1800 rpm. By applying an external torque of $25 \pi \mathrm{Nm}$ for 40 s, the speed increases to 2100 rpm. The diameter of the disk is ________ m.
solution:
Given, $\mathrm{m}=1 \mathrm{~kg}$
$\begin{aligned}
& \omega_{\mathrm{i}}=1800 \mathrm{rpm}=1800 \times \frac{2 \pi}{60}=60 \pi \frac{\mathrm{rad}}{\mathrm{sec}} \\ & \omega_{\mathrm{f}}=2100 \mathrm{rpm}=2100 \times \frac{2 \pi}{60}=70 \pi \frac{\mathrm{rad}}{\mathrm{sec}} \\ & \tau_{e x t}=25 \pi \mathrm{Nm} \\ & \mathrm{t}=40 \mathrm{sec}
\end{aligned}$
Using equation of motion
$\begin{aligned}
& \omega_{\mathrm{f}}=\omega_{\mathrm{i}}+\alpha \mathrm{t} \\ & 70 \pi=60 \pi+\alpha(40) \\ & \alpha=\frac{\pi}{4} \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{sec}^2
\end{aligned}$
Also, $\tau=\mathrm{I} \alpha$
$\tau=\frac{\mathrm{mR}^2}{4} \alpha$
$\begin{aligned}
& 25 \pi=\frac{1 \times \mathrm{R}^2}{4} \times \frac{\pi}{4} \\ & \mathrm{R}=20 \mathrm{~m}
\end{aligned}$
Hence, diameter of disk $=2 \mathrm{R}=2 \times 20=40 \mathrm{~m}$

subject: Physics
q:
A solid cylinder length is suspended symmetrically through two massless strings, as shown in the figure. The distance from the initial rest position, the cylinder should be unbinding the strings to achieve a speed of 4 m s-1, is _____cm.

(take g=10 m s-2)
question:
A solid cylinder length is suspended symmetrically through two massless strings, as shown in the figure. The distance from the initial rest position, the cylinder should be unbinding the strings to achieve a speed of 4 m s-1, is _____cm.

(take g=10 m s-2)
solution:
The portion of the strings between the ceiling and the cylinder are at rest. Hence, the points of the cylinder where the strings leave it are at rest. The cylinder is thus rolling without slipping on the strings. 

Now, from energy conservation

mgh=12mv2+12Iω2

where, I is moment of inertia of cylinder and ω=vR is angular velocity.

Then, we have

 mgh=12mv2+12mR22ω2⇒gh=12v2+12R22vR2

10h=162+164⇒h=1.2 m=120 cm

q: A stationary horizontal disc is free to rotate about its axis. When a torque is applied on it, its kinetic energy as a function of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">θ</mi></math>, where <math><mi mathvariant="normal">θ</mi></math> is the angle by which it has rotated, is given as <math><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">θ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>. If its moment of inertia is <math><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math> then the angular acceleration of the disc is:
question: A stationary horizontal disc is free to rotate about its axis. When a torque is applied on it, its kinetic energy as a function of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">θ</mi></math>, where <math><mi mathvariant="normal">θ</mi></math> is the angle by which it has rotated, is given as <math><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">θ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>. If its moment of inertia is <math><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math> then the angular acceleration of the disc is:
solution:
Kinetic Energy =kθ2
12Iω2=kθ2
ω2=2kθ2I
Differentiate both side w.r.t. θ .
2ωdωdθ=4kθI
ωdωdθ=2kθI
α=2kθI

question: A block is simply released from the top of an inclined plane as shown in the figure above. The maximum compression in the spring when the block hits the spring is :

question: A rod of linear mass density ' $\lambda$ ' and length ' $L$ ' is bent to form a ring of radius 'R'. Moment of inertia of ring about any of its diameter is :
solution:
$L=2 \pi R$
$\mathrm{I}=\frac{\mathrm{MR}^2}{2}=\frac{\lambda \times \mathrm{L}}{2} \times\left(\frac{\mathrm{L}}{2 \pi}\right)^2=\frac{\lambda \mathrm{L}^3}{8 \pi^2}$

subject: Physics
q: A ring and a solid sphere roll down the same inclined plane without slipping. They start from rest. The radii of both bodies are identical and the ratio of their kinetic energies is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>7</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></mfrac></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></math> is ______.
question: A ring and a solid sphere roll down the same inclined plane without slipping. They start from rest. The radii of both bodies are identical and the ratio of their kinetic energies is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>7</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></mfrac></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></math> is ______.
solution:
In pure rolling work done by friction is zero. Hence, potential energy is converted into kinetic energy. Since initially the ring and the sphere have same potential energy, finally they will have same kinetic energy too.
∴ Ratio of kinetic energies =1
⇒7x=1⇒x=7

subject: Physics
q: A uniform solid cylinder of length $L$ and radius $R$ has moment of inertia about its axis equal to $I_{1}$. A small co-centric cylinder of length $L / 2$ and radius $R / 3$ carved from this cylinder has moment of inertia about its axis equals to $I_{2}$. The ratio $I_{1} / I_{2}$ is $\_\_\_\_$.
question: A uniform solid cylinder of length $L$ and radius $R$ has moment of inertia about its axis equal to $I_{1}$. A small co-centric cylinder of length $L / 2$ and radius $R / 3$ carved from this cylinder has moment of inertia about its axis equals to $I_{2}$. The ratio $I_{1} / I_{2}$ is $\_\_\_\_$.
solution:
For solid cylinder: $I = \frac{1}{2}mR^2$
Large cylinder: mass $m_1 = \rho \pi R^2 L$, so $I_1 = \frac{1}{2}\rho \pi R^2 L \times R^2 = \frac{\rho \pi R^4 L}{2}$
Small cylinder (carved out): length L/2, radius R/3, mass $m_2 = \rho \pi(R/3)^2(L/2) = \frac{\rho \pi R^2 L}{18}$
$I_2 = \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{\rho \pi R^2 L}{18} \times (R/3)^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{\rho \pi R^2 L}{18} \times \frac{R^2}{9} = \frac{\rho \pi R^4 L}{324}$
$\frac{I_1}{I_2} = \frac{\rho \pi R^4 L/2}{\rho \pi R^4 L/324} = \frac{324}{2} = 162$

we know that by se the parallel axis theorem :-

Moment of inertia about a point which is parallel of the axis  and at a distance of  X given as 

Ix=Icm+mx2

Ix=25mR2+mx2  ⇒  Parabola opening upward

q: A mass M hangs on a massless rod of length l which rotates at a constant angular frequency. The mass M moves with steady speed in a circular path of constant radius. Assume that the system is in steady circular motion with constant angular velocity ω. The angular momentum of M about point A is LA which lies in the positive z direction and the angular momentum of M about B is LB. The correct statement for this system is:
question: A mass M hangs on a massless rod of length l which rotates at a constant angular frequency. The mass M moves with steady speed in a circular path of constant radius. Assume that the system is in steady circular motion with constant angular velocity ω. The angular momentum of M about point A is LA which lies in the positive z direction and the angular momentum of M about B is LB. The correct statement for this system is:
options: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>L</mi><mi>A</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>L</mi><mi>B</mi></msub></math> are both constant in magnitude and direction
<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>L</mi><mi>B</mi></msub></math> is constant in direction with varying magnitude
<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>L</mi><mi>B</mi></msub></math> is constant, both in magnitude and direction
<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>L</mi><mi>A</mi></msub></math> is constant, both in magnitude and direction
solution:
We know, L→=m(r→×v→)

Now with respect to A, we always get direction
of L→ along +ve z-axis and also constant magnitude as mvr. But with respect to B, we get constant magnitude but continuously changing direction.

subject: Physics
q:
A circular disk of radius R meter and mass M kg is rotating around the axis perpendicular to the disk. An external torque is applied to the disk such that $\theta(t)=5 t^2-8 t$, where $\theta(t)$ is the angular position of the rotating disc as a function of time $t$.
How much power is delivered by the applied torque, when $t=2 \mathrm{~s}$ ?
question:
A circular disk of radius R meter and mass M kg is rotating around the axis perpendicular to the disk. An external torque is applied to the disk such that $\theta(t)=5 t^2-8 t$, where $\theta(t)$ is the angular position of the rotating disc as a function of time $t$.
How much power is delivered by the applied torque, when $t=2 \mathrm{~s}$ ?
solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \theta=5 \mathrm{t}^2-8 \mathrm{t} \\
& \omega=\frac{\mathrm{d} \theta}{\mathrm{dt}}=10 \mathrm{t}-8 \\
& \alpha=\frac{\mathrm{d} \omega}{\mathrm{dt}}=10 \\
& \therefore \mathrm{p}=\tau \omega \\
& =(\mathrm{I} \alpha) \omega \\
& =\left(\frac{\mathrm{mR}^2}{2}\right) \alpha \omega \\
& =\left(\frac{\mathrm{mR}^2}{2}\right)(10)(10
\end{aligned}$

Put $\mathrm{t}=2$
$\mathrm{p}=60 \mathrm{mR}^2$

I=Mr2=ML2π2

subject: Physics
q: Two identical solid spheres each of mass 2 kg and radii 10 cm are fixed at the ends of a light rod. The separation between the centres of the spheres is 40 cm. The moment of inertia of the system about an axis perpendicular to the rod passing through its middle point is______×10–3 kg m2.
question: Two identical solid spheres each of mass 2 kg and radii 10 cm are fixed at the ends of a light rod. The separation between the centres of the spheres is 40 cm. The moment of inertia of the system about an axis perpendicular to the rod passing through its middle point is______×10–3 kg m2.
solution:
The moment of inertia of a sphere is given by the formula I=25MR2.

Since there are two masses, the total moment of inertia about the axis perpendicular to the rod passing through the centre is 

I=2×25MR2+Ml2+r2   ...(i)

The given data is 

M=2 kgl=0.4-0.2 m=0.2 mR=0.1 m

Substituting the values in equation (i)

I=45(2×0.12)+2×2(0.22+0.1)2  kg m2⇒I=45×0.02+4×0.04  kg m2⇒I=0.016+0.16  kg m2=0.176 kg m2

subject: Physics
q: Shown in the figure is rigid and uniform one meter long rod AB held in horizontal position by two strings tied to its ends and attached to the ceiling. The rod is off mass 'm' and has another weight of mass 2 m hung at a distance of 75 cm from A. The tension in the string at A is:
question: Shown in the figure is rigid and uniform one meter long rod AB held in horizontal position by two strings tied to its ends and attached to the ceiling. The rod is off mass 'm' and has another weight of mass 2 m hung at a distance of 75 cm from A. The tension in the string at A is:
solution:
Net torqueτnet aboutB  is zero at equilibrium

TA×100-mg×50-2mg×25=0

TA×100=100×mg

∴TA=1 mg.

a 1 + α R = a    →    (1)

subject: Physics
q: A thin circular ring of mass $\mathrm{m}$ and radius $\mathrm{R}$ is rotating about its axis with a constant angular velocity $\omega$. Two objects each of mass $M$ are attached gently to the opposite ends of a diameter of the ring. The ring now rotates with an angular velocity $\omega^{\prime}=$
question: A thin circular ring of mass $\mathrm{m}$ and radius $\mathrm{R}$ is rotating about its axis with a constant angular velocity $\omega$. Two objects each of mass $M$ are attached gently to the opposite ends of a diameter of the ring. The ring now rotates with an angular velocity $\omega^{\prime}=$
solution:
$L_i=L_f$
$m R R^2 \omega=\left(m R^2+2 M R^2\right) \omega^{\prime}$
$\omega^{\prime}=\left(\frac{m \omega}{m+2 M}\right)$

q: A force <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>F</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mo>(</mo><mover><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mover><mi>j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="italic">k</mi><mo mathvariant="italic">^</mo></mover><mo>)</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math> acts at a point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mn>4</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="italic">i</mi><mo mathvariant="italic">^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="italic">j</mi><mo mathvariant="italic">^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mover><mi>k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>)</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. Then the magnitude of torque about the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mover><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="italic">j</mi><mo mathvariant="italic">^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="italic">k</mi><mo mathvariant="italic">^</mo></mover><mo>)</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mi>x</mi><mo> </mo></msqrt><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>.</mo></math>The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is..........
question: A force <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>F</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mo>(</mo><mover><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mover><mi>j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="italic">k</mi><mo mathvariant="italic">^</mo></mover><mo>)</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math> acts at a point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mn>4</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="italic">i</mi><mo mathvariant="italic">^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="italic">j</mi><mo mathvariant="italic">^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mover><mi>k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>)</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. Then the magnitude of torque about the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mover><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="italic">j</mi><mo mathvariant="italic">^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="italic">k</mi><mo mathvariant="italic">^</mo></mover><mo>)</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mi>x</mi><mo> </mo></msqrt><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>.</mo></math>The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is..........
solution:
r→=4-1i^+3-2j^+-1-1k^

=3i^+j^-2k^

τ=r→×F→=i^jk^31-2123

=i^7-j^11+k^5=7i^-11j^+5k^

=49+121+25=195

subject: Physics
q: The magnitude of torque on a particle of mass $1 \mathrm{~kg}$ is 2.5 Nm about the origin. If the force acting on it is $1 \mathrm{~N},$ and the distance of the particle from the origin is $5 \mathrm{~m},$ the angle between the force and the position vector is (in radians):
question: The magnitude of torque on a particle of mass $1 \mathrm{~kg}$ is 2.5 Nm about the origin. If the force acting on it is $1 \mathrm{~N},$ and the distance of the particle from the origin is $5 \mathrm{~m},$ the angle between the force and the position vector is (in radians):
solution:
Torque about the origin $=\vec{\tau}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}$

$=r F \sin \theta \Rightarrow 2.5=1 \times 5 \sin \theta$

$\sin \theta=0.5=\frac{1}{2}$

$\Rightarrow \theta=\frac{\pi}{6}$

question: In the given figure, two wheels P and Q are connected by a belt B. The radius of P is three times that of Q. In the case of the same rotational kinetic energy, the ratio of rotational inertias I1I2 will be x: 1 . The value of x will be ______.
solution:
12I1ω12=12I2ω22

I1v3R2=I2vR2

I1I2=91

subject: Physics
q: A thin uniform rod of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>, cross sectional area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and density <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi></math> is rotated about an axis passing through the centre and perpendicular to its length with angular velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ω</mi></math>. If value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ω</mi></math> in terms of its rotational kinetic energy <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mfrac><mrow><mi>α</mi><mi>E</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>A</mi><mi>d</mi></mrow></mfrac></msqrt></math>, then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> is ______.
question: A thin uniform rod of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>, cross sectional area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and density <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi></math> is rotated about an axis passing through the centre and perpendicular to its length with angular velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ω</mi></math>. If value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ω</mi></math> in terms of its rotational kinetic energy <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mfrac><mrow><mi>α</mi><mi>E</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>A</mi><mi>d</mi></mrow></mfrac></msqrt></math>, then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> is ______.
solution:
Rotational kinetic energy is given by E=12Iω2, here, I is moment of inertia of rod about an axis through its centre and perpendicular to it.

So, E=12ml212ω2

⇒E=12dAl312ω2    ∵mass=density×volume

⇒E=dA(2)324ω2

⇒ω=3EdA

Thus, the value of α=3.

q: The time dependence of the position of a particle of mass <math><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn></math> is given by <math><mi> </mi><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover></math> . Its angular momentum, with respect to the origin, at time <math><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi></math> is:
question: The time dependence of the position of a particle of mass <math><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn></math> is given by <math><mi> </mi><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover></math> . Its angular momentum, with respect to the origin, at time <math><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi></math> is:
solution:
r→=2t i^-3t2 j^
v→=2 i^-6t j^
Momentum p→=mv→
=4 i^-12t j^
∴ angular momentum L→=r→×p→
=2t i^-3t2 j^×4 i^-12t j^
=-24t2 k^+12t2 k^
=-12t2 k^
At time t=2
L→=-48 k^
